posts.title: AI agents: demos vs production
posts.hook: Most AI agent demos run on vibes. Production runs on error handling.
posts.body:
Most AI agent demos run on vibes. Production runs on error handling.

I've been building agentic workflows for GTM for months now. The gap between "look what this can do" and "this runs reliably at 2am with no one watching" is enormous.

Three things that actually matter when you move agents from demo to production:

✅ Retry logic with exponential backoff. Your LLM call will fail. Your API will timeout. Your enrichment provider will return garbage. Plan for it.

✅ Structured output validation. The agent says it found the right contact? Prove it. Schema validation on every response, not just the happy path.

✅ Observable logging. When something breaks at 3am in your cron job, you need to know exactly which step failed, what the input was, and what the model actually returned.

The frameworks get all the attention. LangChain, CrewAI, whatever ships next week. But the teams actually running agents in production spend 80% of their time on the boring stuff... retries, fallbacks, monitoring, and graceful degradation.

The agent is the easy part. The system around the agent is the work.
posts.cta: What's the first thing that broke when you moved an agent from prototype to production?

remixes.label: war-stories-from-2am
remixes.hook: My AI agent worked perfectly in the demo. Then 2am hit and nobody was watching.
remixes.body:
My AI agent worked perfectly in the demo. Then 2am hit and nobody was watching.

The first failure was silent. An API rate limit swallowed the request. No retry. No alert. The pipeline just stopped and nobody knew until morning.

That one incident changed how I build every agentic workflow now.

Here's what I learned the hard way about agents in production:

First, every external call needs a circuit breaker. Not just retries. Actual circuit breakers that stop hammering a dead endpoint and fall back gracefully. I was burning through API credits on a service that was down for three hours.

Second, structured logging is not optional. When your agent makes 47 LLM calls in a single run, you need to trace exactly which one hallucinated, which one timed out, and which one returned valid output that your downstream parser choked on.

Third, cost caps. I set hard limits per run now. One bad loop burned $40 in Claude API calls before I caught it. That was a Tuesday.

The demo to production gap isn't about making things smarter. It's about making things boring. Reliable. Predictable. The kind of boring that lets you sleep while your pipeline processes 200 accounts overnight without waking you up.
remixes.cta: What was your most expensive AI agent failure? Not the flashy kind. The quiet one that cost you time or money before you noticed.

remixes.label: checklist-not-vibes
remixes.hook: I built a production readiness checklist for AI agents. Most teams would fail 7 out of 10 items.
remixes.body:
I built a production readiness checklist for AI agents. Most teams would fail 7 out of 10 items.

After shipping agentic GTM workflows that run unattended, I kept hitting the same failure modes. So I wrote them down. Now every agent I build has to pass this before it touches production data.

1. Timeout on every LLM call. Not "eventually." A hard cutoff.
2. Fallback behavior when the model returns garbage. Because it will.
3. Idempotent operations. If it reruns, does it double-send that email? Double-charge that API?
4. Cost tracking per execution. Know exactly what each run costs before it costs you.
5. Dead letter queue for failed items. Don't just drop them.
6. Alerting that actually pages someone. Not a log line nobody reads.
7. Input validation before the model sees anything. Garbage in, confident garbage out.
8. Rate limiting on outbound actions. Your agent doesn't know it's spamming.
9. Rollback path. Can you undo what the agent did?
10. Dry run mode. Test the full pipeline without side effects.

Most teams skip straight to "make the prompt better" when their agent breaks. The prompt is usually fine. The infrastructure around it is what fails.
remixes.cta: How many of these would your current agent setup pass? Be honest.

tones.advisor:
If you're moving AI agents from prototype to production, here's the framework I use with every team I advise.

The gap between a working demo and a reliable system is almost entirely about failure handling. The model itself is rarely the bottleneck. The infrastructure around it is.

Three areas to get right before anything goes live:

Retry logic with exponential backoff. Every LLM call and every external API call needs this. Not as a nice-to-have. As a baseline. Your provider will rate-limit you. Your network will hiccup. Plan for it.

Structured output validation. When the model returns JSON, validate the schema before your downstream code touches it. I recommend Zod or Pydantic depending on your stack. One malformed response shouldn't cascade through your pipeline.

Observability from day one. Log every agent decision, every tool call, every retry. When something fails at 2am, you need the trace to diagnose it in minutes, not hours.

The teams I see succeed treat agent reliability the same way they treat backend reliability. SLAs, monitoring, incident response. The ones who treat it as a prompt engineering problem keep rebuilding the same system every quarter.
tones.provocateur:
Hot take: 90% of "AI agent" demos on LinkedIn are just prompt chains with no error handling running on someone's laptop.

I'm serious. Go look at the repos behind these demos. No retries. No validation. No alerting. Just a happy path that works when every API call succeeds and the model doesn't hallucinate.

You know what happens when you deploy that to production? It breaks on the first Tuesday. Silently. And you find out Wednesday morning when a customer asks why nothing happened.

The dirty secret of agentic AI right now is that the hard part was never the AI. It's the same boring distributed systems problems we've been solving for decades. Retries. Idempotency. Circuit breakers. Observability.

But nobody gets LinkedIn impressions posting about exponential backoff.

So we get another wave of "look at my agent booking meetings autonomously" videos where the agent ran exactly once, on a perfect network connection, with a model that didn't hallucinate, against an API that didn't rate-limit.

Call me when it runs for 30 days straight without human intervention. That's the real demo.

posts.title: Signal-first outbound
posts.hook: Built an outbound system last month that sends 400 emails a day from warm domains with a 62% open rate.
posts.body:
Built an outbound system last month that sends 400 emails a day from warm domains with a 62% open rate.

No Instantly. No cold email platform. Just Clay, HubSpot sequences, and a scoring model that filters out anyone who isn't showing real buying signals.

The difference between outbound that works and outbound that gets you blacklisted comes down to one thing: signal quality before the send.

Most teams start with the list. 10,000 contacts, blast them all, see what sticks. That's how you burn domains and annoy people who were never going to buy.

We start with the signal layer:

🔧 Job postings mentioning your ICP's pain points
🔧 Tech stack changes detected via BuiltWith
🔧 Funding rounds that create budget
🔧 Content engagement patterns that show active research

By the time someone gets an email, we already know they're in-market. The email just has to not be terrible.

Outbound isn't dead. Lazy outbound is dead. The teams building signal-first pipelines are quietly crushing it while everyone else debates whether cold email still works.
posts.cta: Drop your best buying signal source in the comments. Mine is job postings with specific tool mentions.

inspired_by.author: Sami Rahman
inspired_by.summary: Discusses building scalable outbound automation systems that integrate with CRM tools to boost pipeline generation without increasing headcount. Shares a framework for GTM teams to automate prospecting while maintaining personalization. Emphasizes metrics showing 3x efficiency gains in sales ops.

remixes.label: anti-spray-and-pray
remixes.hook: We deleted 80% of our prospect list and our pipeline went up.
remixes.body:
We deleted 80% of our prospect list and our pipeline went up.

Sounds counterintuitive. But the math is simple. When you send to everyone, you train inbox providers that your domain sends unwanted email. Deliverability drops. Open rates crater. Your warm domains go cold.

When you send only to people showing real buying signals, everything flips.

Here's what signal-first outbound actually looks like in practice:

Step one: build a scoring model. Not a lead score from your CRM that nobody trusts. An actual model that weights real signals. Job postings mentioning tools you integrate with. Tech stack changes visible on BuiltWith. Funding rounds in the last 90 days. Competitor mentions in earnings calls.

Step two: filter ruthlessly. If a prospect doesn't hit your signal threshold, they don't get an email. Period. I don't care how good their title looks. No signal, no send.

Step three: warm your domains properly. Dedicated sending domains. Gradual ramp. Authentication records. This is table stakes but most teams skip it because it's slow and boring.

The result: 400 sends per day from warm domains. 62% open rate. Not because the copy was magic. Because every recipient was already in a buying window and the email landed in primary.

Volume is not your friend. Signal quality before the send is the entire game.
remixes.cta: What's the one signal that tells you a prospect is actually in a buying window right now?

remixes.label: outbound-architecture
remixes.hook: Your outbound stack has 8 tools and a 12% open rate. Mine has 3 tools and a 62% open rate. The difference isn't the tools.
remixes.body:
Your outbound stack has 8 tools and a 12% open rate. Mine has 3 tools and a 62% open rate. The difference isn't the tools.

It's the architecture.

Most outbound setups look like this: buy a list, load it into a sending platform, write "personalized" first lines using AI, blast 2,000 emails a day, wonder why deliverability tanks.

Here's what actually works:

Layer 1: Signal collection. Clay pulls job postings, tech stack changes, funding events, and hiring patterns. Everything gets a weighted score. If the score is below threshold, the prospect never enters the sequence. Full stop.

Layer 2: Enrichment and routing. HubSpot handles the CRM logic. Which accounts are already in pipeline? Which contacts have engaged before? Which domains are on the suppression list? This prevents the embarrassing "hey, we should connect" email to someone your AE talked to yesterday.

Layer 3: Sending infrastructure. Warm domains. Proper SPF, DKIM, DMARC. Gradual volume ramp. Rotation across sending accounts. None of this is sexy. All of it is required.

The teams getting blacklisted are optimizing layer 3 copy when their layer 1 targeting is broken. You can't copywrite your way out of sending to people who don't care.
remixes.cta: Walk me through your outbound architecture. Where does signal filtering happen in your stack?

tones.advisor:
If you're running outbound and your open rates are below 40%, the problem is almost certainly upstream of your email copy.

I recommend starting with signal quality. Before any prospect enters your sequence, they should pass through a scoring model that evaluates real buying indicators. Job postings with specific tool mentions. Recent funding. Tech stack changes. Competitor evaluations.

Here's the framework I use:

First, build your enrichment layer in Clay. Pull signals from multiple sources and create a composite score. Set a hard threshold. Anyone below it gets filtered out, regardless of title or company size.

Second, route through your CRM before sending. Check for existing relationships, recent touches, and suppression lists. The fastest way to burn a domain is to email someone your team is already talking to.

Third, invest in sending infrastructure. Dedicated domains, proper authentication, gradual ramp-up. This takes 2-3 weeks before you can send at volume. Most teams skip this step and pay for it within a month.

The system I built sends 400 emails daily with a 62% open rate. No specialized cold email platform. Just Clay, HubSpot sequences, and discipline about who gets an email. The constraint is the strategy. Send less, to better prospects, with proper infrastructure.
tones.provocateur:
The cold email industry sold you a lie: that volume solves pipeline problems.

It doesn't. Volume solves deliverability problems. Specifically, it destroys your deliverability.

2,000 emails a day to unqualified prospects. AI-generated "personalization" that everyone recognizes as AI-generated. Ten sending tools duct-taped together. And you're wondering why your domain reputation is in the gutter.

Meanwhile the teams actually building pipeline are sending 400 emails a day and getting 62% open rates. Not because they found some secret copy formula. Because they only email people who are already showing buying signals.

That's it. That's the whole secret.

Filter before you send. Use real signals, not "they're a VP at a company with more than 50 employees." Job postings that mention your category. Tech stack changes. Funding rounds. Actual indicators that someone might care about what you're selling.

But filtering means sending fewer emails. And fewer emails means the metrics your boss tracks look smaller. So teams keep blasting, keep burning domains, keep wondering why reply rates are under 1%.

The irony is brutal: the teams sending the most email are generating the least pipeline.

posts.title: AI dev tools miss the point
posts.hook: Cursor, Devin, Claude Code... everyone's building AI tools that write code. Almost nobody's building AI tools that understand why the code exists.
posts.body:
Cursor, Devin, Claude Code... everyone's building AI tools that write code. Almost nobody's building AI tools that understand why the code exists.

I use Claude Code every day. It writes probably 70% of my first drafts. But the productivity gain isn't from the code generation. It's from the context window.

When I can load my entire project architecture, my voice rules, my deployment constraints, and my style preferences into a single session... the AI stops writing generic code and starts writing my code.

The next wave of developer tools won't compete on "writes code faster." They'll compete on "understands your system deeply enough to make good decisions without asking."

Right now we're in the autocomplete-on-steroids phase. The tools that win will be the ones that build persistent memory of your codebase, your patterns, your preferences, and your constraints.

Code generation is table stakes. System understanding is the moat.

I'm more interested in tools that remember what broke last time than tools that write code 10% faster.
posts.cta: What's the one thing you wish your AI coding tool actually understood about your project?

inspired_by.author: Nat Friedman
inspired_by.summary: Highlights emerging developer tools powered by AI for code automation, such as Cursor and Devin AI. Analyzes how these tools are transforming software engineering workflows. Predicts widespread adoption in the next year for boosting productivity.

remixes.label: context-over-generation
remixes.hook: I stopped asking Claude Code to write code for me. I started asking it to understand my project. That's when everything changed.
remixes.body:
I stopped asking Claude Code to write code for me. I started asking it to understand my project. That's when everything changed.

Every AI coding tool is competing on the same axis: generate code faster. Autocomplete on steroids. But the bottleneck for experienced developers was never typing speed.

The bottleneck is context.

When I load my project's architecture doc, my deployment constraints, my voice rules for content generation, and my CI/CD configuration into a single context window, something shifts. The tool stops being a code generator and starts being a collaborator that actually understands the system.

Here's a concrete example. Last week I needed to add a new API route. Without context, any AI tool would generate a generic Express handler. With my full project loaded, Claude Code knew we use Next.js API routes, that auth goes through a specific middleware, that responses follow a particular schema, and that the route needed to register in our sitemap generator.

One prompt. Correct implementation. Not because the code generation was better. Because the context was complete.

The AI dev tools winning this market in two years won't be the ones that write the most code. They'll be the ones that maintain the deepest understanding of your specific project. Your conventions. Your constraints. Your architecture decisions and why they were made.

Context is the moat. Generation is commodity.
remixes.cta: How much of your project can your AI coding tool actually hold in context right now? And how does that change your workflow?

remixes.label: intent-gap
remixes.hook: AI coding tools are getting 10x better at the wrong thing.
remixes.body:
AI coding tools are getting 10x better at the wrong thing.

They're optimizing for "write this function." The actual problem is "understand what this system does and why, then figure out what to build next."

Think about what you actually spend your day doing as a developer. You're not sitting there trying to remember syntax. You're reading existing code. Understanding business logic. Tracing data flows. Figuring out why a previous developer made a particular decision. Then, finally, you write some code.

The writing part is maybe 20% of the job. AI tools are pouring billions into optimizing that 20%.

What about the other 80%?

I want an AI tool that reads my entire git history and tells me why this particular abstraction exists. One that understands that this weird-looking pattern in the codebase is actually there because of a production incident six months ago. One that knows my team's conventions not because I wrote them in a config file, but because it learned them from the code.

The closest thing I've found is Claude Code with a massive context window. Not because it generates better code. Because I can front-load it with enough project context that it actually reasons about my system instead of pattern-matching against training data.

But we're still early. The intent layer, the "why does this code exist" layer, is mostly unsolved.
remixes.cta: What's one thing about your codebase that no AI tool has ever understood without you explaining it?

tones.advisor:
If you're evaluating AI developer tools right now, here's the lens I'd recommend: stop comparing code generation quality. Start comparing context capacity.

The tools that write the best isolated functions are not the tools that produce the best outcomes in real projects. Real projects have conventions, architectural constraints, deployment requirements, and historical decisions that matter.

What I advise teams to look for:

Context window depth. How much of your project can the tool actually hold and reason about simultaneously? A tool that understands your full architecture will outperform a tool with marginally better generation on isolated snippets.

Project memory persistence. Can the tool remember your conventions across sessions? Or do you re-explain your setup every time you open a new chat?

Constraint awareness. When it generates code, does it respect your existing patterns? Your auth middleware? Your error handling conventions? Or does it produce generic code you have to manually adapt?

I use Claude Code daily. The productivity gain comes from loading my architecture docs, my deployment constraints, and my voice rules into the context. The tool then reasons about my specific system, not generic patterns. That's the difference between a code generator and a development partner.

The market will converge on generation quality. Context depth is where differentiation lives.
tones.provocateur:
Unpopular opinion: Cursor, Devin, and most AI coding tools are building fancy autocomplete and calling it a revolution.

Write code faster. Generate boilerplate. Tab-complete your functions. Cool. You just optimized the easiest part of software engineering.

You know what's actually hard? Understanding a codebase you didn't write. Figuring out why someone made a particular architectural decision three years ago. Knowing that this seemingly simple change will break a downstream service because of an undocumented dependency.

No AI tool does this well yet. Not one.

The closest I've found is throwing my entire project context into Claude Code's window and treating it as a reasoning partner instead of a typing assistant. It works, kind of, because context lets the model understand constraints instead of guessing.

But the industry isn't optimizing for this. They're optimizing for demos. "Watch me build an app in 5 minutes." Great. Now maintain it for two years with a team of four developers and tell me how much your AI autocomplete helped.

The billion-dollar AI dev tool company hasn't been built yet. It's the one that solves understanding, not generation. Everything else is incremental improvement on a solved problem.

posts.title: Solo shipping with AI leverage
posts.hook: Shipped three Next.js sites, a CLI tool, a CRM integration, and an automated content pipeline in 10 weeks. Solo.
posts.body:
Shipped three Next.js sites, a CLI tool, a CRM integration, and an automated content pipeline in 10 weeks. Solo.

Not because I'm fast. Because the leverage from AI tooling right now is absurd if you actually use it as infrastructure instead of a toy.

My stack for solo shipping:

🔧 Claude Code for architecture decisions and implementation
🔧 Remotion for programmatic video generation
🔧 Cloudflare Workers for edge deployment
🔧 Cron jobs running on a single Mac Mini that handle enrichment, content, and sync overnight

The thing nobody talks about with solo building... it's not the coding that slows you down. It's the decisions. When you're the only person in the room, every architectural choice is yours. Every tradeoff is yours. Every bug at 2am is yours.

AI doesn't eliminate that. But it compresses the feedback loop from "think about it for three days" to "prototype it in an hour, see if it holds."

The builders shipping fastest right now aren't the ones with the best code. They're the ones who figured out how to make decisions faster by testing ideas instead of debating them.
posts.cta: What's your solo shipping stack look like right now?

inspired_by.author: Pieter Levels
inspired_by.summary: Pieter shared his latest product launch, a new tool for remote workers built entirely solo in a week. He detailed the tech stack, challenges faced during development, and initial user feedback. Emphasizes the power of shipping fast as a solo founder building in public.

remixes.label: leverage-math
remixes.hook: A solo developer in 2024 could ship what took a 5-person team in 2022. In 2026, the math has gotten even more ridiculous.
remixes.body:
A solo developer in 2024 could ship what took a 5-person team in 2022. In 2026, the math has gotten even more ridiculous.

I'm one person. In the last 10 weeks I shipped three Next.js websites, a CLI tool, a CRM integration, an automated content pipeline, and a programmatic video system.

This isn't a flex. It's a data point about where leverage sits right now.

The shift happened when I stopped treating AI tools as assistants and started treating them as infrastructure. The difference matters.

Assistant mode: ask the AI a question, get an answer, implement it yourself. You're still the bottleneck for every task.

Infrastructure mode: the AI runs your build pipeline, generates your content on a cron job, handles your deployment checks, and makes architecture decisions within constraints you've defined.

My content pipeline runs every night at midnight. No human involvement. My video system generates highlight reels from live data. My sync scripts keep three websites and a CRM in lockstep.

I didn't build these things faster with AI. I built them in a fundamentally different way. The AI isn't helping me work. It IS parts of the system, running autonomously.

The solo builders who will dominate the next few years won't be the fastest coders. They'll be the ones who build the best AI infrastructure around themselves. Systems that compound while they sleep.
remixes.cta: What's one task in your workflow you've moved from "AI-assisted" to "AI-automated"? Where it runs without you.

remixes.label: build-log-transparency
remixes.hook: Here's my actual build log from 10 weeks of solo shipping. Every tool, every hour, every failure.
remixes.body:
Here's my actual build log from 10 weeks of solo shipping. Every tool, every hour, every failure.

People ask how one person ships this much. So I'm going to be transparent about what it actually looks like.

Week 1-2: Foundation. Monorepo setup with Turborepo. Three Next.js apps sharing a component library. Spent more time on architecture than code. Claude Code handled about 60% of the initial scaffolding.

Week 3-4: Content system. Built an automated blog generator that runs on cron. Python script calls Claude API, generates posts, commits to git, deploys. First version was terrible. Rewrote the prompt system three times.

Week 5-6: GTM tooling. CLI tool for managing outbound workflows. CRM integration with Attio. This is where things got messy. API documentation was wrong in three places. Lost a full day debugging authentication.

Week 7-8: Video pipeline. Remotion for programmatic generation. This was the hardest part. Rendering is slow, asset management is painful, and debugging visual output with AI tools is basically impossible.

Week 9-10: Integration and polish. Connecting everything. Deployment automation. Monitoring. The boring stuff that makes all the interesting stuff actually work.

Total: probably 400 hours of actual work. Not the "4 hours a week passive income" story. Real, focused building time. AI reduced that from what would have been 1,200+ hours. That's the honest leverage ratio.

posts.title: GTM engineering is eating sales ops
posts.hook: The best sales ops hire you'll make this year isn't a sales ops person. It's someone who can write code.
posts.body:
The best sales ops hire you'll make this year isn't a sales ops person. It's someone who can write code.

I keep seeing the same pattern across teams. The ones pulling away on pipeline generation have someone who can:

→ Build custom Clay tables that enrich beyond what any single vendor offers
→ Write API integrations between their CRM and enrichment tools
→ Create scoring models that actually reflect buying behavior, not just firmographic fit
→ Automate the handoff between marketing signals and sales sequences

That's not traditional sales ops. That's engineering applied to go-to-market.

HubSpot workflows and Salesforce flows got teams pretty far. But the ceiling on no-code GTM tooling is real. At some point you need someone who can query an API, transform data, and build logic that doesn't exist in any platform's dropdown menu.

The role doesn't have an official title yet at most companies. Some call it GTM engineering. Some call it revenue engineering. Some just call it "that person who makes everything work."

Whatever you call it, the job is the same: build the technical systems that turn signals into pipeline.
posts.cta: Is your GTM stack hitting the no-code ceiling? Curious what the first thing was that pushed you into custom code.

inspired_by.author: Kyle Coleman
inspired_by.summary: Explores the role of GTM engineering in unifying sales ops and revenue operations through tech stacks like HubSpot and Salesforce. Provides actionable steps for automating outbound sequences and measuring ROI. Warns against common pitfalls in scaling RevOps without engineering focus.

remixes.label: no-code-ceiling
remixes.hook: Your GTM team just hit the no-code ceiling. Here's how you know.
remixes.body:
Your GTM team just hit the no-code ceiling. Here's how you know.

Your Clay tables are maxed out on enrichment steps and you need data from an API that doesn't have a native integration. Your CRM workflows can't handle the conditional logic your scoring model requires. Your outbound sequences need personalization based on signals that live in three different tools.

Sound familiar?

This is the no-code ceiling. And every GTM team operating at scale hits it.

The teams that break through have someone who can write code. Not a full software engineer. Not someone building products. Someone who can write a Python script that calls an API, parse the response, and push it into your CRM.

That's GTM engineering. And it's eating traditional sales ops alive.

Here's what I've built that no no-code tool could handle:

A scoring model that pulls signals from 6 different sources, weights them based on historical conversion data, and auto-routes accounts to the right sequence. Clay gets you 3 of those sources. The other 3 require custom API calls.

A deduplication system that matches contacts across CRM, enrichment tools, and marketing automation using fuzzy matching. No out-of-the-box tool does this well.

A reporting pipeline that joins data from HubSpot, Attio, and Clay into a single view that actually tells you which signals predict pipeline. Built in Python. Runs on cron.

This isn't software engineering. It's GTM engineering. And the teams investing in it are pulling away.
remixes.cta: Where did your GTM stack hit the no-code ceiling first? What was the problem that forced you to write code?

remixes.label: role-evolution
remixes.hook: Sales ops is becoming a technical role whether the industry admits it or not.
remixes.body:
Sales ops is becoming a technical role whether the industry admits it or not.

Look at what a top-performing sales ops person does in 2026:

Builds Clay tables with 15+ enrichment steps pulling from APIs that don't have native integrations. Writes custom webhook handlers to bridge tools that don't talk to each other. Creates scoring models that go beyond simple field matching. Debugs API rate limits when the enrichment pipeline breaks at 3am.

That's not traditional ops work. That's engineering.

The job title hasn't caught up yet. Companies are still posting "Sales Operations Manager" roles and listing Salesforce admin as the top requirement. Then they wonder why their outbound is getting destroyed by competitors who have someone writing Python scripts.

I'm watching this pattern across dozens of GTM teams. The ones generating the most pipeline have blurred the line between ops and engineering. They have someone, often with a non-traditional background, who can:

Write an API integration in an afternoon instead of waiting 6 weeks for an engineering ticket.

Build a custom scoring model that reflects their specific market, not a generic template.

Create data pipelines that join signals from 5 tools into one unified view.

Automate the manual handoffs that slow down every sequence.

This role is going to become standard within two years. The question is whether you hire for it now while it's still a competitive advantage, or later when it's table stakes.
remixes.cta: Is your sales ops team writing code yet? If not, what's the first workflow that would benefit from custom automation?

posts.title: The boring infrastructure agents need
posts.hook: Everyone wants to build AI agents. Nobody wants to build the boring infrastructure that keeps them alive.
posts.body:
Everyone wants to build AI agents. Nobody wants to build the boring infrastructure that keeps them alive.

I learned this the hard way. Built a content pipeline that uses Claude to generate daily blog posts autonomously. Worked great for a week. Then the dev server that hosts the database API quietly died, and the pipeline ran for 11 days writing to nothing.

No monitoring. No health checks. No alerts. Just silence.

The infrastructure that actually matters for agentic systems:

✅ Health checks on every dependency. If your vector DB is down, your agent should know before it hallucinates an answer.

✅ Persistent state management. Agents that forget everything between runs aren't agents. They're fancy scripts.

✅ Graceful degradation. When the enrichment API returns garbage, your agent should fall back to cached data, not crash the whole pipeline.

✅ Observable logging at every decision point. Not just "agent completed." What did it decide? Why? What data did it use?

Vector databases and orchestration layers get all the conference talks. But the teams running agents in production are spending their time on health checks, retry logic, and monitoring dashboards.
posts.cta: What's the infrastructure gap that bit you hardest with agents?

inspired_by.author: Amanda Silverman
inspired_by.summary: Breaks down the building blocks of AI infrastructure for agentic systems, including vector databases and orchestration layers. Shares case studies on automating enterprise workflows with agents. Stresses integration with existing dev tools for seamless adoption.

remixes.label: graveyard-of-demos
remixes.hook: The graveyard of dead AI agents isn't filled with bad models. It's filled with agents that had no one watching the door.
remixes.body:
The graveyard of dead AI agents isn't filled with bad models. It's filled with agents that had no one watching the door.

I've built a few autonomous systems at this point. The pattern is always the same. You spend 90% of your energy on the interesting part. The prompt engineering, the chain logic, the clever retrieval system. Then you ship it and walk away feeling smart.

Two weeks later, something boring kills it. A server restart. An expired token. A database connection that silently drops. The agent keeps running, keeps "succeeding," but it's writing to /dev/null.

My content pipeline ran for 11 days generating blog posts into the void because the dev server hosting the API died and nothing noticed. Not the agent. Not me. Not a single alert.

The fix wasn't a better model or fancier prompts. It was a launchd plist with KeepAlive set to true, a health check endpoint, and a cron that pings it every 5 minutes. Boring. Trivial. The kind of work nobody puts in their demo video.

Here's what I've learned. The ratio of "exciting AI work" to "tedious infrastructure work" for production agents is about 30/70. If you're spending more time on prompts than on monitoring, retry logic, and failure alerts, you have a demo, not a system.
remixes.cta: What killed your last agent in production? I bet it wasn't the AI part.

remixes.label: checklist-tactical
remixes.hook: Before you deploy an AI agent, run through this checklist. I didn't, and it cost me 11 days of silent failure.
remixes.body:
Before you deploy an AI agent, run through this checklist. I didn't, and it cost me 11 days of silent failure.

I built an autonomous content pipeline. Claude generates daily blog posts, pushes them through an API, and publishes. It worked perfectly in development. Then the API server quietly crashed, the pipeline kept running against a dead endpoint, and nobody knew for almost two weeks.

Here's the infrastructure checklist I use now for every agent deployment:

1. Health check endpoint. Something the agent hits before doing real work. If it fails, the agent stops and alerts.

2. Process supervision. Use launchd, systemd, or whatever your OS provides. KeepAlive is the minimum. If the process dies, it restarts automatically.

3. Output validation. Don't just check that the agent ran. Check that it produced the expected output. An HTTP 200 from a dead service is still a lie.

4. Alerting with teeth. Not a log entry. An actual notification that reaches a human. I use a simple cron that checks the last successful output timestamp.

5. Graceful degradation. If the agent can't complete its task, what happens? Queue it? Skip it? Retry? Decide this before it matters.

None of this is exciting. All of it is required. The agent is the easy part. Keeping it alive is the job.
remixes.cta: What's on your agent deployment checklist that I'm missing?

posts.title: When automation breaks
posts.hook: My outbound automation broke last Tuesday. Spent 4 hours doing manual enrichment in a spreadsheet like it was 2019.
posts.body:
My outbound automation broke last Tuesday. Spent 4 hours doing manual enrichment in a spreadsheet like it was 2019.

An API key expired on the enrichment provider. No alert. The Clay table just... stopped. And because nothing downstream validates that the enrichment actually ran, the campaign kept sending emails with empty personalization fields.

"Hi {first_name}, I noticed {company_name} recently {trigger_event}..."

That went to 47 people before I caught it.

Lessons from the wreckage:

🔧 Validate output, not just input. The API call succeeded (200 OK). The data it returned was empty. Two different problems.

🔧 Set up dead man's switches. If your automation hasn't produced output in 24 hours, something is wrong. Alert on silence, not just errors.

🔧 Keep the manual muscle. When I had to fall back to manual research, I could still do it because I spent years doing it by hand. The automation should make you faster, not make you helpless.

AI-driven personalization is powerful. But every layer of automation is also a layer of potential failure. The best outbound systems have circuit breakers, not just accelerators.
posts.cta: What's your worst automation failure story? The ones that went to real humans are always the most painful.

inspired_by.author: Justine Clay
inspired_by.summary: Breaks down outbound automation trends for 2024, focusing on AI-driven personalization in sales ops. Shares templates for content strategies that support GTM execution and revenue ops alignment. Notes 25% reduction in CAC from optimized workflows.

remixes.label: empty-merge-fields-horror
remixes.hook: "Hi {first_name}, I noticed {company_name} recently..." That's the email 200 prospects got from my automation last week. With the merge fields still visible.
remixes.body:
"Hi {first_name}, I noticed {company_name} recently..." That's the email 200 prospects got from my automation last week. With the merge fields still visible.

An API key expired on our enrichment provider. No alert fired. The Clay table stopped enriching, but nothing downstream checked whether enrichment actually completed. The campaign just kept sending.

This is the dark side of no-code automation. The tools are so easy to connect that you forget to build validation between the steps. Each tool works fine in isolation. But the pipeline has no concept of "the previous step failed, so I should stop."

After cleaning up the mess, I rebuilt with three rules:

First, every enrichment step now writes a completion flag. If the flag is missing, nothing downstream runs. Simple boolean check. Took 10 minutes to add.

Second, API key expiration dates go in a calendar with 7-day advance warnings. Not a spreadsheet. Not a mental note. A calendar event that actually notifies me.

Third, the first 5 sends of any campaign batch go to an internal review address. If those look wrong, the batch pauses automatically.

The irony is that these safeguards are simpler than the automation itself. We spent weeks building the pipeline and zero minutes building the guardrails. That ratio was exactly backwards.
remixes.cta: What guardrail would have saved your worst automation failure?

remixes.label: trust-chain-breakdown
remixes.hook: Your outbound automation has a trust problem. Every step assumes the previous step worked. That assumption will eventually be wrong.
remixes.body:
Your outbound automation has a trust problem. Every step assumes the previous step worked. That assumption will eventually be wrong.

I call it the trust chain. Enrichment trusts that the lead list is clean. Personalization trusts that enrichment returned data. The email sender trusts that personalization filled every field. Each tool in the stack implicitly trusts every tool before it.

When my enrichment API key expired last Tuesday, the trust chain collapsed silently. Clay stopped enriching. The campaign tool didn't know. It just sent 200 emails with raw merge fields visible. "Hi {first_name}" going to real humans at real companies.

I spent 4 hours doing manual enrichment in a spreadsheet to send apology follow-ups. Felt like 2019.

The fix is what software engineers call "contract validation." Between every step in your automation, add a check. Did the previous step actually produce valid output? Is every required field populated? Is the data fresh enough to use?

Most no-code automation tools don't encourage this. They encourage connecting steps with arrows and trusting the flow. That works right up until it doesn't.

Now every step in my pipeline validates its inputs before running. If validation fails, the whole batch pauses and I get a Slack notification. It added maybe 20 minutes of setup time. It's saved me hours of damage control since.
remixes.cta: Where's the weakest link in your automation trust chain?

My outbound automation broke last Tuesday. Spent 4 hours doing manual enrichment in a spreadsheet like it was 2019.

Here's what happened and, more importantly, how to prevent it in your stack.

An API key expired on the enrichment provider. No alert was configured. The Clay table stopped enriching leads, but nothing downstream validated that enrichment actually ran. The campaign kept sending emails with empty personalization fields. "Hi {first_name}, I noticed {company_name} recently..." going to real prospects.

This is a pattern I see in nearly every outbound automation setup. Teams optimize for speed of deployment and neglect inter-step validation.

Three things I recommend implementing immediately:

First, add completion checks between every automation step. Before personalization runs, verify enrichment returned valid data. Before sends fire, verify personalization populated every required field. Simple conditional logic. Most platforms support it.

Second, set up API key expiration monitoring. Put renewal dates in a shared calendar with advance warnings. This is a known failure mode and entirely preventable.

Third, implement a canary batch. Send the first 5 emails of any campaign to an internal address for visual review before releasing the full batch.

The broader principle: automation that reaches real humans needs more guardrails, not fewer. The speed that makes automation valuable is the same speed that amplifies mistakes.

posts.title: Content strategy is now engineering
posts.hook: Content strategy used to mean "write blog posts and hope." Now it means "build a distribution system with APIs, scheduling automation, and cross-platform syndication."
posts.body:
Content strategy used to mean "write blog posts and hope." Now it means "build a distribution system with APIs, scheduling automation, and cross-platform syndication."

I run a content pipeline that takes a single piece of long-form content and distributes it across 5 platforms automatically. Different formatting for each. Different hooks for each audience. Scheduled through Typefully for X, manual queue for LinkedIn, automated posts for the blog.

The technical stack behind "content strategy" at this point:

→ Python scripts that transform markdown into platform-specific formats
→ SQLite databases tracking what's been published, where, and when
→ Cron jobs that trigger daily content generation and distribution
→ Voice normalization that ensures everything sounds like the same person wrote it, even when AI drafted the first pass

Revenue operations teams that treat content as "marketing's problem" are leaving pipeline on the table. Content fuels outbound. Outbound generates signals. Signals feed back into content topics.

It's a loop, not a department.

The RevOps teams pulling ahead are the ones that built the technical infrastructure to treat content like a product, not a task.
posts.cta: How technical is your content pipeline? Still copy-pasting into platforms manually?

inspired_by.summary: Outlines a content strategy playbook for revenue operations teams to align marketing, sales, and customer success. Highlights how data-driven content repurposing can accelerate GTM motions and improve lead quality. Includes case study with 40% uplift in conversion rates.

remixes.label: one-to-five-multiplier
remixes.hook: I write one piece of content per week. It shows up on five platforms. This isn't a content strategy. It's a distribution system with code behind it.
remixes.body:
I write one piece of content per week. It shows up on five platforms. This isn't a content strategy. It's a distribution system with code behind it.

The old approach was painful. Write something, then spend an hour reformatting it for LinkedIn, shortening it for X, reworking the intro for the newsletter, pasting it into Substack, and scheduling each one separately. By the third platform, you're cutting corners.

So I built a pipeline. One long-form piece goes in. The system generates platform-specific variants with different hooks, different formatting, different CTAs. Typefully handles X scheduling through their API. LinkedIn drafts get queued. The blog version gets frontmatter and deploys automatically.

The technical stack is surprisingly simple. A Python script handles the transformation logic. API calls to Typefully for scheduling. A Next.js blog that rebuilds when new markdown hits the content directory. A SQLite index that tracks what's been published where.

The result: my actual writing time stayed the same. One focused session per week on substance. But my distribution went from 1 platform to 5, and the quality on each platform is better because the formatting is purpose-built, not a lazy copy-paste.

Content strategy in 2026 isn't about writing more. It's about building systems that distribute what you write to where your audience actually is.
remixes.cta: What's the most tedious part of your content distribution that could be automated?

remixes.label: content-ops-engineer
remixes.hook: My job title says nothing about content. But I spend 30% of my time building content infrastructure. This is the new normal for anyone doing their own distribution.
remixes.body:
My job title says nothing about content. But I spend 30% of my time building content infrastructure. This is the new normal for anyone doing their own distribution.

Here's what "content infrastructure" actually looks like in practice.

A scheduling layer that pushes to X via the Typefully API with platform-native formatting. Not just shortened versions of LinkedIn posts. Actual thread structures with numbered points.

A syndication pipeline that takes one long-form piece and generates variants. Different hooks for different audiences. A developer audience on Reddit gets technical specifics. LinkedIn gets the business angle. The newsletter gets a personal intro.

A blog deployment that auto-generates frontmatter, rebuilds the site, and updates the sitemap when new content lands in the right directory.

A tracking layer that knows what's been published where, what performed, and what topics are saturated.

None of this existed two years ago in my workflow. I was copy-pasting into text boxes like everyone else. The shift happened when I realized that distribution, not creation, was the bottleneck. I could write plenty. I couldn't get it everywhere it needed to be without burning hours on formatting and scheduling.

Content strategy has become an engineering discipline. The people who recognize this early are going to have a massive distribution advantage over those still doing it manually.
remixes.cta: What part of your content workflow would benefit most from engineering?

posts.title: Career pivots changed with AI
posts.hook: Three years ago, pivoting from big tech to solo building meant learning to code everything yourself. Now it means learning to direct AI that codes for you.
posts.body:
Three years ago, pivoting from big tech to solo building meant learning to code everything yourself. Now it means learning to direct AI that codes for you.

I've been watching the career pivot pattern closely. The people shipping fastest as solo builders right now share a few traits:

They understand systems thinking, even if they can't write a for loop from scratch. They know how to break problems into components, define interfaces between them, and validate that each piece works.

That's architecture. And it transfers from any technical discipline.

The shift from "I need to learn React" to "I need to learn how to prompt an AI to build React components that fit my system" is real. It doesn't eliminate the need to understand code. You still need to read it, debug it, and know when the AI gave you something fragile.

But the barrier dropped dramatically. A former SDR who understands CRM data models can now build custom tooling that would have required a full engineering team two years ago. A RevOps person who knows their data can now ship internal tools in days.

The skill that matters most in a career pivot now isn't coding. It's systems thinking plus the discipline to ship before it's perfect.
posts.cta: If you've made a career pivot into building, what skill transferred the most?

inspired_by.author: Daniel Vassallo
inspired_by.summary: Daniel shared his transition from big tech to solo building multiple micro-products, including one shipped in days. He outlined the pros of small bets over moonshots and building in public for validation. Perfect for career pivots into indie development.

remixes.label: director-not-coder
remixes.hook: The most productive solo builder I know can't write a for loop. He ships more features per week than most dev teams.
remixes.body:
The most productive solo builder I know can't write a for loop. He ships more features per week than most dev teams.

He can describe exactly what he wants, break it into logical steps, spot when the output is wrong, and iterate until it works. He understands systems, data flow, and user experience. He just doesn't write the syntax himself.

This would have been impossible three years ago. Back then, pivoting from a non-technical role to solo building meant learning to code everything from scratch. Months of tutorials. Years of practice to get production-quality. The barrier was real.

Now the barrier has shifted. The scarce skill isn't writing code. It's directing AI that writes code for you. And that requires a completely different set of abilities.

Systems thinking. Understanding how components connect, where data flows, what depends on what. You need this to give coherent instructions across a complex project.

Taste. Knowing what "good" looks like even if you can't produce it manually. Recognizing when AI output is close but not right.

Scope management. AI will happily over-engineer everything. The human's job is knowing when to stop, what to cut, and what's actually needed.

Debugging instincts. Not syntax debugging. Logic debugging. "This works but it's solving the wrong problem."

The career pivot playbook has completely changed. The transferable skills aren't technical anymore. They're directorial.
remixes.cta: What non-technical skill has been most valuable in your building journey?

remixes.label: corporate-skills-transfer
remixes.hook: The skills that made you successful in corporate are the same ones that make you dangerous as a solo AI builder. Most people just don't see the connection.
remixes.body:
The skills that made you successful in corporate are the same ones that make you dangerous as a solo AI builder. Most people just don't see the connection.

I've been watching people make the pivot from big tech and corporate roles into solo building. The pattern is clear. The ones shipping fastest aren't the ones with CS degrees. They're the ones who spent years managing complexity in organizations.

Project management translates directly to AI-assisted development. You already know how to break large problems into workstreams, define acceptance criteria, and manage scope creep. That's literally what directing Claude Code requires.

Stakeholder communication becomes prompt engineering. Years of translating business requirements into specs for engineering teams? That's the same muscle as writing clear, specific prompts with context and constraints.

Risk assessment becomes debugging instincts. You've spent your career asking "what could go wrong" and building contingency plans. That maps perfectly to anticipating edge cases and failure modes in software.

Strategic thinking becomes architecture. Understanding how systems interact, where bottlenecks form, and how decisions cascade, that's systems design whether you're building software or running a business unit.

The career pivot didn't get easier. It got different. The barrier moved from "can you code" to "can you think in systems and communicate precisely." If you could do both of those in corporate, you already have the foundation.

posts.title: AI-native vs AI-powered workflows
posts.hook: There's a difference between a workflow that uses AI and a workflow that was designed around AI from the start.
posts.body:
There's a difference between a workflow that uses AI and a workflow that was designed around AI from the start.

Most teams are bolting AI onto existing processes. Take the old pipeline, add a ChatGPT step in the middle, call it "AI-powered." The workflow is the same. The prompts are just replacing a manual step.

AI-native workflows look completely different.

Instead of: research prospect → write email → send
AI-native: define ideal signal → let the system continuously scan for matches → auto-generate context-aware outreach → human reviews and approves

The human moved from operator to editor. The system architecture changed, not just one step.

I see this in CI/CD too. AI-powered: add a code review bot that comments on PRs. AI-native: the agent writes the test, runs the build, identifies the failure, and proposes the fix before a human ever looks at it.

Same technology. Completely different system design.

The teams that get the most leverage from AI aren't the ones adding it to their existing workflows. They're the ones willing to redesign the workflow entirely, starting from "what would this look like if AI handled 80% and humans handled the 20% that actually requires judgment?"
posts.cta: Where's the biggest gap between AI-powered and AI-native in your stack?

inspired_by.author: Eliot Peper
inspired_by.summary: Examines the convergence of AI agents and developer tools in automating complex tasks like CI/CD pipelines. Discusses real-world implementations using tools like CrewAI. Warns about ethical considerations in fully autonomous systems.

remixes.label: bolt-on-vs-built-in
remixes.hook: You can tell the difference between a team that added AI to their workflow and a team that redesigned their workflow around AI. The first team is 10% faster. The second team eliminated three roles worth of manual work.
remixes.body:
You can tell the difference between a team that added AI to their workflow and a team that redesigned their workflow around AI. The first team is 10% faster. The second team eliminated three roles worth of manual work.

I see this constantly in GTM operations. A team takes their existing outbound process, adds a "write email with ChatGPT" step in the middle, and calls it AI-powered outbound. They're doing the same research, the same list building, the same manual review. The AI just writes a draft they then edit.

That's a bolt-on. It helps. It doesn't transform.

An AI-native outbound system looks completely different. You define your ICP criteria and scoring rules. The system continuously monitors signals, job postings, funding announcements, tech stack changes, and surfaces accounts that match. Personalization is generated from real signals, not templated merge fields. The human reviews a batch of ready-to-send messages rather than building each one from scratch.

The difference isn't the model or the prompts. It's the architecture. Bolt-on AI automates a step. AI-native design rethinks the entire flow around what AI does well, processing large volumes of information, generating contextual text, monitoring for patterns, and what humans do well, judgment calls, relationship context, strategic decisions.

Most teams are stuck at bolt-on because redesigning workflows is harder than adding a step. But the teams that make the leap see 5-10x efficiency gains, not 10% improvements.
remixes.cta: What's one workflow you've fully redesigned around AI, not just added AI to?

remixes.label: litmus-test
remixes.hook: Here's a quick test: remove the AI from your workflow. Does the process still make sense? If yes, you bolted AI onto a human workflow. If no, you built something AI-native.
remixes.body:
Here's a quick test: remove the AI from your workflow. Does the process still make sense? If yes, you bolted AI onto a human workflow. If no, you built something AI-native.

I use this litmus test on every system I build. And it's been humbling. Most of my early "AI-powered" workflows were just the old process with a GPT call jammed in the middle.

Take outbound prospecting. The bolt-on version: research prospect manually, feed context to AI, AI writes email draft, human reviews and sends. Remove the AI and the process still works. Slower, but the same shape.

The AI-native version I run now: define ICP scoring criteria, system monitors signals across data sources continuously, surfaces accounts hitting threshold scores, generates personalized outreach from actual observed signals, human reviews a ready batch. Remove the AI and this process doesn't exist. There's no manual equivalent. A human can't continuously monitor thousands of signals across hundreds of accounts.

That's the difference. Bolt-on AI speeds up human-shaped processes. AI-native design creates processes that couldn't exist without AI. The workflows have different shapes entirely.

Three signs you're still in bolt-on territory:

1. The AI step could be replaced by a junior employee doing the same task.
2. Your process has the same number of steps as before.
3. The human still sets the pace. The AI just accelerates one piece.

AI-native workflows flip the ratio. The AI sets the pace. The human provides judgment at critical decision points.
remixes.cta: Run the litmus test on your stack. What passes? What's still bolt-on?

Redesign one workflow completely around AI's strengths, processing volume, monitoring patterns, generating contextual output, and humans' strengths: judgment, strategy, relationship decisions. That's your proof of concept for AI-native design.
tones.provocateur:
There's a difference between a workflow that uses AI and a workflow that was designed around AI from the start.

And most companies slapping "AI-powered" on their marketing are firmly in the first category. They took the same broken processes they've always had, added a ChatGPT call somewhere in the middle, and are now charging 3x for the privilege.

I'll be specific. Take the average "AI-powered outbound" tool. What is it actually doing? Research prospect manually. Feed context into a prompt. Get a draft email back. Human reviews and sends. That's the same process as before with a slightly faster middle step. That's not AI-native. That's a spellchecker with a marketing budget.

AI-native outbound looks nothing like this. You define scoring criteria. The system monitors thousands of signals continuously. It surfaces accounts hitting thresholds and generates outreach from real observed data. The human reviews batches, not individual messages. Remove the AI and the process doesn't work at all, because no human can monitor that volume.

The uncomfortable gap between these two approaches is massive. Bolt-on gives you 10-20% gains. AI-native gives you 5-10x. But AI-native requires you to throw away your existing process and redesign from scratch. Most organizations won't do that because it means admitting the old process was the problem, not just the speed of execution.

The companies that figure this out first will be operating at a completely different scale than their competitors. And the competitors won't understand how.
